What’s more, Shopify is encouraging employees to:
🐯Engage with internal communities to adopt AI (hey, that sounds like TIGER Tech Leaders!)

Google just made AI agents accessible to everyone at work, no coding required.
Google launched Gemini Enterprise this week, and it's a game-changer for how teams can automate their workflows.
Here's what matters:
Instead of waiting on IT or hiring developers, any employee can now build custom AI agents using a simple drag-and-drop interface. Think of it as giving your team superpowers to automate repetitive tasks, answer customer questions, or pull insights from company data.
Why this is significant:
→It democratizes AI. Anyone can create solutions for their specific pain points.
→Security is baked in. It connects to your internal data and tools with enterprise-like controls, so you're not sacrificing protection for convenience.
→You don't start from scratch. Google included prebuilt agents for common tasks like research and customer support.
The business case:
Starting at $21-30/user/month, this positions Google squarely against Microsoft and Amazon in the enterprise AI race. For companies looking to scale AI adoption without massive IT investments.
Bottom line: How quickly you can empower your people to use it.

We decided to start using AI tools long before we began offering AI services. I felt we needed to lead by example. We had to understand what AI is and how it can be used before making recommendations to others. We use AI tools internally to make ourselves more efficient, to refine how we work, how to present ourselves, and how to consolidate our knowledge so we can distill the best pieces to offer. We also use these tools to organize information in a way that helps us move forward more effectively.
I read an article where the CEO of Shopify said that moving forward, if someone wanted to hire a new employee, they would need to justify that by showing the job couldn’t be done through AI. I really liked that perspective. I want my team to use AI responsibly but without fear, to see it as a benefit for our company and our clients.
I’m not saying AI does our work. What I’m saying is that it helps us shape things better and identify our blind spots, because we all have them. And since these tools are objective, they can point out those blind spots so we can transform and improve. That’s a theme for us: to be better, both for each other and for our clients.

Retail redefined - Walmart's vision on workflow:
Walmart isn't just dabbling in AI; they're deploying sophisticated "AI agents" to automate repetitive coding tasks, identify accessibility issues, and significantly speed up development cycles. This isn't about replacing developers, but empowering them to shift from mundane tasks to higher-value, more creative problem-solving. It's a clear signal that even the largest enterprises are recognizing the transformative power of AI for internal efficiency and accelerating innovation. As Walmart's CEO, Doug McMillon, puts it, AI is set to "change literally every job."
Our take? This move by Walmart exemplifies a critical paradigm shift. We're moving beyond AI as merely a predictive tool or a customer service chatbot. We're entering an era where AI becomes an active, agentic participant in the creation process itself, fundamentally redefining human-computer collaboration in the workplace. This isn't just about faster code; it's about unlocking human potential by offloading the programmatic grunt work.
What implications does this widespread adoption of AI agents by major corporations have for the global talent landscape and the skill sets required for tomorrow's workforce? What changes are you planning to make?

They sell AI as the ultimate solution to make businesses smarter, faster, and more efficient. But when it comes to their own systems, that intelligence is missing. Their customer support chatbots cannot understand context. They cannot solve basic problems. They repeat scripted answers and loop users into endless automated replies.
It feels less like talking to intelligence, and more like arguing with a wall. The irony is glaring.
The very companies preaching AI transformation are struggling to use it within their own ecosystems. If the creators of AI cannot deploy it effectively to assist their customers, what exactly are they selling to us?

For MSME owners and small businesses who are constantly told that AI will revolutionize their operations, this is a wake up call. The first time it hit me personally was in 2023 with AWS. I needed support for our cloud setup and realized I now had to pay to speak to a human being. I even thought the support was complimentary, until the bill arrived. I reached out to a few people in my circle, and they confirmed they had to pay for customer service support too. On Shopify and Meta too. Want customer service? You’ll meet automated replies, AI bots, or blog articles. The only way to reach a human is through the paid verification package. Some platforms still give free access, for now, but they’ll switch eventually. Like I always say, everything isn’t being replaced; most things are being reinvented. Customer service is one of them. Anything requiring human touch will stay in demand. The more automation we experience, the more people will crave human connection, voice, empathy, and presence. Human support is now a premium. For businesses, this is a new revenue model. AWS has scaled it. Shopify and Meta are doing it. Airlines, banks, telcos, and heavy-demand service providers will follow. It won’t work immediately for everyone. Some customers will revolt at first. But it’ll normalize, packaged as a VIP tier, lifestyle support, or premium helpdesk.
